content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 27,
1995
Ms.
Debra A. Greenfield
General Counsel
San Diego Association of Governments
Suite 800, First Interstate Plaza
401
B Street
San Diego, California
92101
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-95-079
Dear Ms. Greenfield:
You have asked for confirmation of telephone advice on behalf
of the San Diego Association of Governments under the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May the Chairman of the San Diego Association of Governments
(“SANDAG”),
who is an elected official,
sign a letter to
approximately 2,000 residents of the region asking them to
participate in a travel behavior study?
CONCLUSION
The Chairman of
SANDAG,
who is also the Mayor of Imperial
Beach,
may sign a letter requesting 2,000 residents of the region
to participate in a travel survey since
SANDAG is required to
maintain an up—to—date travel model for the area,
conducting the
survey is essential to maintaining an up—to—date model,
and the
agency determined that the Chairman’s signature was necessary to
obtain residents’ participation in the time—consuming survey.
FACTS
SANDAG is a joint powers agency established pursuant to
Section 6500 et seq.
of the Government Code,
whose membership
consists of all the cities within the county and the entire County
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—079
Page
2
of San Diego.
All of the members of the Board of Directors are
either mayors,
councilmembers or supervisors.
SANDAG is primarily
funded by federal, state,
and local government sources.
SANDAG serves as the Metropolitan Planning Organization and
the Regional Transportation Planning Agency for purposes of
carrying out the required federal and state mandates in order for
the region to receive federal and state transportation funds.
Conducting the transportation survey is necessary to the planning
program to carry out those mandates.
The purpose of the survey is to collect travel behavior
characteristics of residents to update the transportation models.
The use of up—to-date models is required for planning projects.
About 2,000 surveys will be mailed.
The survey is being conducted
for
SANDAG and Caltrans by a survey research firm.
The funding
source for the work related to this survey,
and the survey itself,
is federal and state transportation funds.
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 of the Act provides that “[n]o newsletter or
other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense.”
A “mass
mailing” means “over two hundred substantially similar pieces of
mail, but does not include a form letter or other mail which is
sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other
inquiry.”
(Section 82041.5.)
Commission regulations provide that
a mass mailing at public expense is prohibited if allof the
following criteria are met:
(1)
Any item sent is delivered,
by any means, to
the recipient at his or her residence, place of employ
ment or business,
or post office box.
For purposes of
this subdivision
(a) (1), the item delivered to the
recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape,
record,
or button,
or a written document.
(2)
The item sent either:
(A)
Features an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing,
or
(B)
Includes the name, office, photograph,
or
other reference to an elected officer affiliated with
the agency which produces or sends the mailing,
and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, co
ordination,
or concert with the elected officer.
(3) (A)
Any of the costs of distribution is paid
for with public moneys; or


File No.
A—95—079
Page
3
(B)
Costs of design, production,
and printing
exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys,
and the
design, production,
or printing is done with the intent
of sending the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.
(4)
More than two hundred substantially similar
items are sent,
in a single calendar month,
excluding
any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision
(b).
(Regulation 18901(a).)
The cover letter to the transportation survey,
signed by
SANDAG’s chairman, the Mayor of Imperial Beach, falls within the
above definition of a mass mailing.
However,
an exception to the
prohibition on mass mailings at public expense exists for
essential agency communications.
Regulation 18901(b) (6)
states:
Any item sent by an agency responsible for
administering a government program,
to persons subject
to that program,
in any instance where the mailing of
such item is essential to the functioning of the
program, where the item does not include the elected
officer’s photograph;
and where use of the elected
officer’s name,
office, title,
or signature is necessary
to the functioning of the program.
As a joint powers agency established under Government Code
Section 6500 et seq.,
SANDAG is a local government agency.
Section 82003 defines “agency” to include any state or local
government agency.
Section 82041 defines “local government
agency” to mean a county, city,
or district of any kind including
school district,
or any other local or regional political
subdivision,
or any department, division,
bureau, office,
board,
commission or other agency of the foregoing.
A joint powers
agency is considered a local government agency under the Act.
(See Alperin Advice Letter,
No.
1-94-177 and Siegel Advice Letter,
No.
A—81--015.)
SANDAG acts as the Metropolitan Planning Organization and the
Regional Transportation Planning Agency for San Diego.
It carries
out the federal and state mandates necessary for the region to
receive federal and state transportation funds.
To carry out
these functions,
SANDAG is required to have an up—to—date travel
model.
Conducting the transportation survey is essential to the
agency’s maintaining an up—to—date travel model and carrying out
its overall planning functions.
The cover letter to the survey
does not have a picture of an elected official.
The letter is
signed by the Mayor of Imperial Beach,
in his capacity as Chairman
of
SANDAG.
Completing the travel survey is somewhat burdensome
since it requires residents to track and report on their


File No.
A—95—079
Page 4
transportation use over a period of time.
The agency determined
that the Chairman’s signature was necessary to encourage residents
to participate in and complete the survey.
Therefore, the above
exception for essential agency communications would apply.
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
p. LyLi
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel,
Legal Division

(“SANDAG”),
who is an elected official,
sign a letter to
approximately 2,000 residents of the region asking them to
participate in a travel behavior study?

CONCLUSION: The Chairman of
SANDAG,
who is also the Mayor of Imperial
Beach,
may sign a letter requesting 2,000 residents of the region
to participate in a travel survey since
SANDAG is required to
maintain an up—to—date travel model for the area,
conducting the
survey is essential to maintaining an up—to—date model,
and the
agency determined that the Chairman’s signature was necessary to
obtain residents’ participation in the time—consuming survey.
